does: This paper proposes a preference-based reinforcement learning algorithm that directly optimizes policies from human preference comparisons without learning an intermediate reward model. The method uses a contrastive learning framework to design a novel policy scoring metric that assigns higher scores to policies aligning with given preferences. The algorithm is evaluated on offline RL tasks with actual human preference labels, demonstrating performance that either matches or exceeds existing two-stage preference-based methods, with particularly strong results on high-dimensional control tasks where it surpasses standard offline RL approaches.
connects: This work builds on the established line of preference-based reinforcement learning, which emerged as an alternative to explicit reward specification when reward function design is intractable. Prior PbRL methods followed a standard pipeline of first fitting a reward model from preference data, then applying conventional RL algorithms—an approach inherited from inverse RL and reward learning literature. The paper directly responds to the challenge that reward models can be inaccurate when trained solely from preference signals, especially human preferences. It combines insights from contrastive learning frameworks with policy optimization, extending recent work on learning from comparisons and building on advances in offline RL.
enables: This direct optimization approach opens avenues for more robust preference-based learning systems that avoid reward model errors propagating through the RL pipeline. The method could enable better scaling of human feedback in RL by eliminating the intermediate modeling stage, potentially making preference-based learning more practical for complex tasks where accurate reward specification is infeasible. The contrastive scoring framework may generalize to other settings combining preference data with policy learning, and the approach suggests that policy space methods might more naturally accommodate preference information than traditional reward-centric formulations. Future work could explore this framework with weaker preference signals, larger-scale human feedback collection, and deployment in interactive settings where policy learning and preference gathering occur jointly.
